pathophysiology_summary: Chronic acne vulgaris driven by androgen-stimulated sebum production, follicular hyperkeratinisation, Cutibacterium acnes colonisation and perifollicular inflammation. In this flare, deep nodulocystic lesions have ruptured, releasing keratin and lipid into the dermis and provoking an intense local inflammatory/foreign-body response with reactive perilesional erythema and focal purulence — this mimics facial cellulitis. Key discriminators: presence of comedones and deep cysts, truncal involvement, absence of a sharply demarcated advancing erythematous plaque, absence of systemic toxicity, and only a low-grade reactive temperature. The important-not-to-miss diagnosis is facial/periorbital cellulitis, which is actively excluded here by the screening negatives (no high fever/rigors, no spread toward the eye, no visual change, no advancing border or lymphadenopathy).

differential.evidence_basis: Deep painful cysts on a background of comedones/oily skin with truncal involvement — most consistent picture.
Severe pain plus perilesional erythema flagged on phone triage; must be screened for and excluded before reassurance.
Pustular facial eruption can be folliculitis, but comedones and deep cysts point to acne.
Facial papulopustules, but comedones and truncal spread argue against rosacea.

differential_progression.scoring_note: AI Doctor must keep facial/periorbital cellulitis as important-not-to-miss and screen for it; must not accept 'infection spreading' without the negatives, nor dismiss severity.

differential.evidence_basis: Comedones + deep cysts + truncal involvement with screening negatives for cellulitis/sepsis confirm inflammatory acne.
No high fever/rigors, no spread toward the eye, no visual change, no advancing demarcated border — cellulitis excluded clinically.
Comedones and truncal involvement are inconsistent with rosacea.
Working diagnosis: urgent-primary-care management with oral + topical therapy and clear safety-net for cellulitis features.

red_flags.description: High fever (>38.5°C) with rigors / systemic toxicity
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate secondary bacterial cellulitis or systemic spread requiring escalation. Absent — only low-grade 37.6°C.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If truly present and missed, evolving cellulitis/sepsis could be under-treated.

red_flags.description: Erythema spreading toward the periorbital region or any visual change
red_flags.clinical_significance: Periorbital/orbital cellulitis is sight- and life-threatening; must be actively excluded. Absent here.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed periorbital/orbital cellulitis can progress to vision loss or cavernous sinus thrombosis.

red_flags.description: Focal purulent nodulocystic lesions with reactive perilesional erythema and severe pain
red_flags.clinical_significance: Marks a severe inflammatory flare at high risk of hypertrophic scarring — warrants prompt oral antibiotic + topical therapy even without systemic infection.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Phone triage said 'possible spreading infection'. AI Doctor anchors on cellulitis and escalates to ED/IV antibiotics despite screening negatives — over-triage.
Equating severe pain with serious infection and missing that severe inflammatory acne is itself very painful.
Opposite failure — dismissing as 'just acne' without screening for periorbital spread or arranging follow-up.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation for fluctuance and to define lesion borders
Whether erythema has a distinct advancing/demarcated edge
Reliable core temperature without a home thermometer
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day in-person clinician review for assessment and prescription; escalate to ED/000 only if periorbital spread, visual change, high fever or rigors develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Treating this as cellulitis and sending to ED for IV antibiotics is over-triage; conversely dismissing it risks missed periorbital cellulitis. The safe path is prompt primary-care acne management with an explicit cellulitis safety-net.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Moderate-to-severe inflammatory acne: combine a topical agent with an oral tetracycline (e.g., doxycycline) unless contraindicated; refer for isotretinoin if severe/scarring.
